chunk_summary: This section defines acute malnutrition in children under 5 in India, outlines severity criteria, and provides guidelines for level-of-care decisions, including outpatient and facility-based management, follow-up, and referral pathways.
learning_objectives: Understand the anthropometric criteria for diagnosing MAM and SAM in children under 5 years.
Learn the level-of-care decision framework for managing acute malnutrition in India.
Identify medical complications that necessitate facility-based care for malnourished children.
Recognize the importance of follow-up and referral systems in community-based malnutrition programs.
key_facts: Moderate Acute Malnutrition (MAM): Weight-for-length/height z-score between -2 and -3 SD.
Severe Acute Malnutrition (SAM): Weight-for-length/height z-score <-3 SD, MUAC <115 mm (6–59 months), or bilateral pitting edema.
Uncomplicated SAM can be managed in the community if a structured program exists; otherwise, refer to a facility.
Infants <6 months who are visibly wasted or too weak to suckle require urgent facility assessment.
Follow-up weekly initially; escalate care if temperature ≥39°C, hypothermia, new edema, or weight loss for two consecutive weeks.
Link eligible families to Anganwadi centres (AWC) under ICDS to reduce loss to follow-up.
reference_quote: Guideline: Prevention and management of wasting and nutritional oedema in infants and children under 5 years — https://www.who.int/publications/i/item
document_key_topics: Definitions and criteria for MAM and SAM
Follow-up and referral systems in India
